narrative:
First Warn Forecast

Tuesday, May 5, 2026 – Happy Cinco de Mayo

First Warn Weather Headlines
Periods of showers and a few thunderstorms continue through this evening
Severe weather and flooding are not expected
Cooler than normal temperatures settle in through Thursday
Gradual warming trend begins late week into the weekend
Today – Tuesday (Cinco de Mayo)

We’re dealing with a fairly classic post-convective, boundary-driven setup across the mid-Mississippi Valley today, and it’s going to show in the form of off-and-on rain for much of the day.

An outflow boundary from last evening’s storms has pushed south through portions of southeast Missouri and southwest Illinois early this morning. That boundary has essentially blurred the location of the true synoptic front, which now appears to be draped just south of the Highway 36/I-72 corridor. What this means for us is that we’re sitting on the cooler side of the boundary, while deeper instability and better severe potential remain displaced to our south.

A persistent southwest flow aloft continues to override this boundary, allowing moisture to ride northward into the region. Embedded within that flow are subtle shortwave disturbances—nothing overly strong individually, but enough to provide just enough lift to keep showers going through much of the day. As we move into the afternoon and evening hours, a slightly more organized disturbance approaches, and that’s when we’ll likely see a more noticeable uptick in shower coverage and a few thunderstorms mixed in.

From a severe weather standpoint, the ingredients simply do not line up locally. Instability remains marginal at best across our area, with most guidance keeping SBCAPE values well under 1,000 J/kg, and in many spots significantly lower due to cloud cover and limited heating. Meanwhile, the better overlap of instability and shear remains well to our south. You might hear a few rumbles of thunder this afternoon, but the atmosphere just doesn’t have the fuel needed to support anything stronger.

Temperatures will reflect the pattern as well. With persistent cloud cover, occasional rain, and weak cold air advection filtering in behind the front, highs will struggle to climb out of the lower 60s—well below what we’d typically expect for early May.

Tonight into Wednesday

As we move into the overnight hours, shower activity will gradually diminish from west to east as the deeper moisture axis shifts and the forcing begins to weaken. That said, lingering clouds will likely hang around, preventing any significant clearing overnight.

By Wednesday, the post-frontal air mass becomes more firmly established across the region. This is a cooler, more stable setup characterized by weak northerly flow at the surface and relatively shallow moisture trapped beneath a lingering cloud deck. The result is another day of mostly cloudy skies and temperatures that once again top out around the low 60s.

Wednesday night will be noticeably chilly for this time of year. With temperatures expected to dip into the upper 30s to near 40 degrees, it’s the kind of night that makes you double-check the calendar. That said, widespread frost concerns remain low. The center of the high pressure system stays displaced to our west, which keeps a light breeze in place and limits the potential for ideal radiational cooling. Additionally, cloud cover may not completely clear out, further reducing the risk.

Extended Outlook

By Thursday, we begin to see the pattern shift—slowly, but in the right direction. The upper-level trough responsible for the recent stretch of unsettled weather slides east, and low- to mid-level ridging begins to build into the region. That should promote at least partial clearing and allow temperatures to rebound slightly, though still running a bit below normal.

Attention then turns to another frontal system expected on Friday. This one is a bit more subtle in terms of temperature change, as the air mass behind it originates more from the Pacific rather than Canada. In other words, it’s not a dramatic cooldown, but it will likely bring another round of scattered showers. There is still some disagreement in the guidance regarding timing, which is why rain chances currently carry some uncertainty—but overall, this looks more like a nuisance rain setup than anything impactful.

Saturday is shaping up to be the pick of the week. Ridging strengthens, skies clear, and temperatures climb back closer to seasonal norms. It’s the kind of day where you finally feel like spring is back on track.

Another shortwave and associated cold front approach on Sunday. This system appears a bit more organized compared to Friday’s, bringing another chance for showers and thunderstorms. However, similar to today’s setup, current guidance keeps the better instability displaced to our south, suggesting that while rain is likely, the risk for strong to severe storms remains very low at this time.

Bottom Line

Today is a cool, cloudy, and occasionally wet day across the region, but it’s also a quiet one from a severe weather standpoint. The atmosphere simply isn’t primed for anything more than scattered showers and a few rumbles of thunder.

It may not be the warm and sunny Cinco de Mayo many would prefer, but it’s also not a day to worry about hazardous weather. Sometimes, a low-key weather day isn’t such a bad thing.

First Warn Weather – First. Accurate. Trusted.
